input: What specific types of metrics and logs does the Amazon EMR integration collect?
actual_output:
**Metrics**: Cluster progress, cluster state, cluster/node storage.  
**Logs**: Cluster status, node status details.
expected_output: The Amazon EMR integration collects two types of data: metrics and logs. Metrics provide insight into the state of Amazon EMR, including cluster progress, cluster state, and cluster or node storage. Logs help keep a record of events happening in Amazon EMR, such as cluster status and node status details.
context:
## Data streams

The AWS NAT gateway integration collects one type of data: metrics.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of your NAT gateways.
Metrics collected by this integration include the number of connection attempts made through the NAT gateway, the total number of concurrent, active TCP connections through the NAT gateway, the number of times the NAT gateway could not allocate a source port, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

## Data streams

The AWS VPN integration collects one type of data: metrics.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of your AWS-hosted VPNs.
Metrics collected by the Amazon VPN integration include the unique host ID, the host IP addresses, the name of the domain of which the host is a member, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

For more details about these requirements, please take a look at the [AWS integration documentation](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/aws#requirements).

## Setup

## Data streams

The Amazon EMR integration collects two types of data: metrics and logs.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of Amazon EMR.
The metrics collected by the Amazon EMR integration include cluster progress, cluster state, cluster or node storage, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference)

**Logs** help you keep a record of events happening in Amazon EMR.
Logs collected by the Amazon EMR integration include the cluster status, node status details and more. 

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

# Amazon EMR (ElasticMapReduce)

The Amazon EMR integration allows you to monitor [Amazon EMR](https://aws.amazon.com/emr/) — a fully managed big data processing and analytics service.

Use the Amazon EMR integration to collect metrics related to your EMR instances. Then visualize that data in Kibana, create alerts to notify you if something goes wrong, and reference the metrics when troubleshooting an issue.

For example, you could use this data to track Amazon EMR cluster progress and cluster storage. Then you can alert when utilization for an instance crosses a predefined threshold.

**IMPORTANT: Extra AWS charges on API requests will be generated by this integration. Check [API Requests](https://www.elastic.co/docs/current/integrations/aws#api-requests) for more details.**

## Data streams

The Amazon EMR integration collects two types of data: metrics and logs.

## Data streams

The Amazon EC2 integration collects two types of data: logs and metrics.

**Logs** help you keep a record of events happening in Amazon EC2.
Logs collected by the Amazon EC2 integration include the region in which an instance is running, the operating system architecture, container information, and more. See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of your Amazon EC2 instances.
Metrics collected by the Amazon EC2 integration include the Amazon EC2 instance ID, the number of earned CPU credits that an instance has accrued since it was launched or started, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

## Data streams

The Amazon ELB integration collects two types of data: logs and metrics.

**Logs** help you keep a record of events happening in Amazon ELB.
Logs collected by the Amazon ELB integration include the time a request was received, the client's IP address, latencies, request paths, server responses, and more. See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of Amazon ELB.
Metrics collected by the Amazon ELB integration include the host name, IP address, average latency, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

input: Given the context provided, how would you interpret the metric aws.natgateway.metrics.PacketsOutToSource.sum in relation to the flow of data within an AWS VPC and what additional metrics might be necessary to fully understand the traffic patterns involving the NAT gateway?
actual_output:
The metric **aws.natgateway.metrics.PacketsOutToSource.sum** tracks the number of packets sent by the NAT gateway back to clients in your VPC (e.g., responses from internet services). It reflects outbound traffic returning to the VPC. 

**Additional metrics for full traffic analysis**:
- **PacketsInFromSource.sum**: Inbound packets from VPC clients (to monitor outbound traffic balance).
- **BytesOutToSource.sum**: Complements packet count with data volume.
- **ConnectionEstablishedCount.sum**: Tracks active connections to correlate with packet flow.
- **ErrorPortAllocation.sum**: Identifies port allocation issues affecting packet transmission.
- **PacketsOutToDestination.sum**: Measures packets sent to external destinations (outbound traffic).
- **IdleTimeoutCount.sum**: Detects connection timeouts impacting packet flow. 

These metrics together provide a holistic view of NAT gateway performance and traffic patterns.
expected_output: The metric aws.natgateway.metrics.PacketsOutToSource.sum represents the total number of packets that have been sent from the NAT gateway back to the clients within the Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). This metric is crucial for understanding the outbound traffic originating from the VPC's instances to external networks and then returning back to the VPC. To fully understand the traffic patterns involving the NAT gateway, additional metrics such as aws.natgateway.metrics.PacketsInFromDestination.sum (the number of packets received by the NAT gateway from external networks) and aws.natgateway.metrics.BytesOutToSource.sum (the amount of data sent out to the source) would be beneficial. These metrics would provide a more comprehensive view of both the volume and direction of traffic handled by the NAT gateway, helping to identify potential bottlenecks, monitor usage, and ensure efficient network performance.

input: What does the 'host.name' field represent, and how does it differ from 'host.hostname'?
actual_output: The `host.name` field represents a flexible host identifier, which can include the hostname, FQDN, or a user-specified name, chosen by the sender. In contrast, `host.hostname` typically reflects the system's `hostname` command output, which may not include the FQDN and is more strictly tied to the machine's configured hostname.
expected_output: The 'host.name' field represents the name of the host, which can include what the `hostname` command returns on Unix systems, the fully qualified domain name, or a name specified by the user. The sender determines which value to use. This field differs from 'host.hostname', which specifically contains what the `hostname` command returns on the host machine, typically without additional domain information.

input: What does 'user_agent.os.full' represent in the context, and how does it differ from 'user_agent.os.name' and 'user_agent.os.version'?
actual_output: 'user_agent.os.full' represents the full operating system name including the version or code name. It differs from 'user_agent.os.name' (which excludes the version) and 'user_agent.os.version' (which provides the version as a raw string).
expected_output: The 'user_agent.os.full' represents the complete name of the operating system used by the user agent, including both the name and the version or code name. It differs from 'user_agent.os.name', which only includes the name of the operating system without any version information, and from 'user_agent.os.version', which only includes the version of the operating system as a raw string.

input: What specific TCP flags does the field 'aws.networkfirewall.tcp.tcp_flags' represent in the context of AWS Network Firewall logs?
actual_output:
The field `aws.networkfirewall.tcp.tcp_flags` represents TCP flags using a bitmask:  
- **SYN** (2)  
- **SYN-ACK** (18)  
- **FIN** (1)  
- **RST** (4)  

These values correspond to the TCP flags tracked in AWS Network Firewall logs.
expected_output: The field 'aws.networkfirewall.tcp.tcp_flags' represents the specific TCP flags that are set in the TCP header of network packets being evaluated by AWS Network Firewall. These flags can include URG (Urgent), ACK (Acknowledgment), PSH (Push), RST (Reset), SYN (Synchronize), and FIN (Finish). However, based on the provided context, only the presence of the RST, SYN, and URG flags are explicitly mentioned as separate boolean fields. The 'tcp_flags' field likely captures the full set of TCP flags, but the exact mapping of its values to specific flags is not detailed in the given information.

retrieval_context:
## Metrics reference

The `firewall_metrics` dataset collects AWS Network Firewall metrics.

An example event for `firewall` looks as following:

# networkfirewall

## Logs

The `networkfirewall` dataset collects Network Firewall logs. Users can use AWS 
Network Firewall logs to monitor network activity.

An example event for `networkfirewall` looks as following:

## Data streams

The AWS Network Firewall integration collects two types of data: logs and metrics.

**Logs** help you keep a record of events happening in AWS Network Firewall.
All three types of logs recorded by [AWS Network Firewall](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/network-firewall/latest/developerguide/firewall-logging.html) can be collected by the integration: `Flow`, `Alert` and `TLS` logs.
Logs collected by the AWS Network Firewall integration include the observer name, source and destination IP, port, country, event type, and more. See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of Network Firewall.
Metrics collected by the AWS Network Firewall integration include the number of packets received, passed, and blocked by the AWS Network Firewall, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

input: Given the context provided, how would you explain the difference between 'aws.rds.throughput.network_transmit' and 'aws.rds.volume.write.iops', and what specific information each metric provides about the performance and usage of an AWS RDS instance?
actual_output:
**aws.rds.throughput.network_transmit** measures outgoing network traffic (bytes/sec) for database communication, including replication and monitoring. It reflects network bandwidth usage.  

**aws.rds.volume.write.iops** tracks write disk I/O operations per second (IOPS) on the storage volume, indicating disk performance under write load.  

**Key Difference**:  
- *network_transmit* focuses on network throughput (data transfer rate).  
- *volume.write.iops* measures disk I/O operations, reflecting storage subsystem workload.  

**Usage Insight**:  
- High *network_transmit* may signal heavy client interaction or replication traffic.  
- High *volume.write.iops* suggests intense write activity, potentially indicating storage bottlenecks.
expected_output:
The metric 'aws.rds.throughput.network_transmit' represents the outgoing (transmit) network traffic on the DB instance, encompassing both customer database traffic and Amazon RDS traffic used for monitoring and replication. This metric is measured in float and presented as a gauge, providing insights into the total data being sent out from the RDS instance to the network. It helps in understanding the bandwidth usage and can be crucial for identifying potential bottlenecks or excessive data transfer costs.

On the other hand, 'aws.rds.volume.write.iops' indicates the number of write disk I/O operations to the cluster volume, reported at 5-minute intervals. This metric is also a gauge but is measured in long integers. It specifically focuses on the write operations performed on the storage volume associated with the RDS instance. By monitoring this metric, one can assess the write performance and workload on the storage layer, which is essential for ensuring efficient data handling and avoiding potential performance issues related to high write activity.

Amazon RDS Performance Insights enables you to monitor and explore different dimensions of database load based on data captured from a running DB instance. DB load is measured as average active sessions. Performance Insights provides the data to API consumers as a two-dimensional time-series dataset. The time dimension provides DB load data for each time point in the queried time range. Each time point decomposes overall load in relation to the requested dimensions, measured at that time point. Examples include SQL, Wait event, User, and Host.

input: Given the context provided, how would you explain the relationship between 'aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg' and 'aws.rds.latency.read', and what insights can be drawn about the performance of disk read operations in Amazon Redshift compared to Amazon RDS?
actual_output: The metric `aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg` measures average disk read I/O latency in Amazon Redshift, while `aws.rds.throughput.read` (assuming typo) reflects average disk I/O latency in Amazon RDS. Both metrics track disk read performance but apply to different services: Redshift (data warehouse) and RDS (general-purpose DB). Redshift's columnar storage and parallel processing may yield lower latency for large-scale analytical workloads, whereas RDS is optimized for transactional use cases. Direct comparison requires contextual data, but Redshift's architecture typically prioritizes throughput over latency for analytics.
expected_output:
The metrics 'aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg' and 'aws.rds.latency.read' both measure the average amount of time taken for disk read I/O operations, but they pertain to different Amazon services: Amazon Redshift and Amazon RDS, respectively. 'aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg' specifically refers to the average time taken for disk read operations in Amazon Redshift, which is a fully managed data warehouse service designed for large-scale data analytics. On the other hand, 'aws.rds.latency.read' measures the average time taken for disk read operations in Amazon RDS, which is a relational database service supporting multiple database engines such as MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server.

By comparing these two metrics, one can gain insights into the performance of disk read operations in Amazon Redshift versus Amazon RDS. If the value of 'aws.redshift.metrics.ReadLatency.avg' is consistently lower than 'aws.rds.latency.read', it may indicate that Amazon Redshift is performing better in terms of disk read efficiency for its specific use case, which is optimized for analytical workloads. Conversely, if 'aws.rds.latency.read' is lower, it could suggest that the RDS instance is more efficient in handling read operations, possibly due to optimizations specific to the database engine being used or the configuration of the RDS instance.

However, it's important to note that direct comparisons between these metrics should consider the specific configurations, workloads, and database engines of each service, as well as any additional factors that might influence disk read performance.

retrieval_context:
## Data streams

The Amazon Redshift integration collects one type of data: metrics.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of Amazon Redshift.
Metrics collected by the Amazon Redshift integration include disk read throughput, storage read latency, query latency, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

For more details about these requirements, please take a look at the [AWS integration documentation](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/aws#requirements).

## Setup

Amazon Redshift Serverless lets you focus on using your data to acquire new insights for your business and customers.

input: Given the context provided, how would you determine which specific Amazon Redshift clusters the 'aws.dimensions.ClusterIdentifier' field is used to filter data for, and what additional information can be inferred about the nature of this filtering?
actual_output: The `aws.dimensions.ClusterIdentifier` field filters data for a specific Amazon Redshift cluster by its unique identifier. This dimension allows precise filtering of metrics (e.g., from CloudWatch) to isolate data tied to that cluster. The value represents the cluster's unique name, ensuring metrics are scoped to that exact cluster instance.
expected_output: The 'aws.dimensions.ClusterIdentifier' field is used to filter data for specific Amazon Redshift clusters by their unique identifiers. This means that if you want to monitor or analyze metrics related to a particular Redshift cluster, you can use this field to specify the cluster's identifier. Additional information that can be inferred about the nature of this filtering is that it allows for granular monitoring at the cluster level, enabling users to isolate and examine metrics for individual clusters without being affected by data from other clusters. This is particularly useful in environments where multiple Redshift clusters are managed and monitored.

input: Given the context provided, how would you determine the significance of the 'aws.route53.firewall.action' field in relation to the DNS Firewall's response to a domain name query, and what additional fields should be considered to fully understand the action taken?
actual_output:
The `aws.route53.firewall.action` field indicates whether a DNS Firewall rule **blocked** or **alerted** on a domain query. To fully understand the action:  
- Check `aws.route53.firewall.domain_list.id` (which domain list triggered the action).  
- Check `aws.route53.firewall.rule_group.id` (which rule group contained the matching rule).  
These fields, combined with the action, provide context on the firewall's response.
expected_output: The 'aws.route53.firewall.action' field indicates the specific action taken by the DNS Firewall in response to a domain name query when a rule match occurs. It can have values such as 'alert' or 'block', indicating whether the DNS Firewall has merely alerted about the query or actively blocked it. To fully understand the action taken, additional fields like 'aws.route53.firewall.domain_list.id' and 'aws.route53.firewall.rule_group.id' should be considered. These fields provide the domain list and rule group IDs that were used to match the domain name in the query, offering insight into the specific rules and policies that led to the action taken by the DNS Firewall.

input: What does the field 'aws.route53.edns_client_subnet' represent in the context of AWS Route 53 DNS logs, and how does it differ from other fields like 'aws.route53.hosted_zone_id'?
actual_output:
The field **`aws.route53.edns_client_subnet`** represents a **partial IP address** of the client that initiated the DNS query, if available via the DNS resolver. It provides subnet-level client location data.  

In contrast, **`aws.route53.hosted_zone_id`** identifies the **specific AWS Route 53 hosted zone** associated with the DNS queries in the log.  

**Key difference**:  
- `edns_client_subnet` focuses on **client network origin** (subnet).  
- `hosted_zone_id` identifies the **DNS zone** managing the queried domain.
expected_output:
The field 'aws.route53.edns_client_subnet' represents a partial IP address of the client that initiated the DNS request, if this information is available from the DNS resolver. This field is useful for identifying the origin of DNS queries, providing insights into the geographic location or network segment of the client.

In contrast, 'aws.route53.hosted_zone_id' refers to the unique identifier of the hosted zone associated with all DNS queries logged in the record. A hosted zone is a collection of DNS records for a domain, and this field helps in associating the DNS queries with a specific domain managed through AWS Route 53. Unlike 'aws.route53.edns_client_subnet', which pertains to the client initiating the query, 'aws.route53.hosted_zone_id' is related to the DNS configuration on the server side (AWS Route 53) and does not provide information about the client's IP address.

input: What does the field 'aws.s3access.total_time' represent in terms of the request's duration from the server's perspective, and how does it differ from 'aws.s3access.turn_around_time'?
actual_output: The field `aws.s3access.total_time` represents the total time the request was in flight from the server's perspective. `aws.s3access.turn_around_time` refers to the time Amazon S3 spent processing the request. The key difference is that `total_time` includes the entire duration (e.g., network latency, processing, etc.), while `turn_around_time` specifically measures the server's processing time.
expected_output: The field 'aws.s3access.total_time' represents the total duration in milliseconds that the request was in flight from the server's perspective, including all aspects of handling the request such as network latency, processing time, and any other delays encountered during the request lifecycle. On the other hand, 'aws.s3access.turn_around_time' specifically measures the duration in milliseconds that Amazon S3 spent processing the request itself, excluding any network-related delays. Essentially, 'total_time' encompasses the entire request handling period from the server's viewpoint, while 'turn_around_time' focuses solely on the processing time within Amazon S3.

input: Given the context provided about the ECS categorization fields, how would you determine the appropriate handling strategy for an event based on its 'event.kind' value, and what specific considerations might influence this decision?
actual_output:
To determine the handling strategy for an event based on `event.kind`, consider:  
1. **Retention**: Different `event.kind` values may require varying data retention policies (e.g., alerts vs. metrics).  
2. **Access Control**: Apply distinct permissions based on the event type.  
3. **Ingestion Frequency**: Assess if data arrives regularly (e.g., metrics) or sporadically (e.g., alerts).  
4. **Processing Prioritization**: Prioritize real-time handling for critical event types.  
The `event.kind` value directly informs these decisions, ensuring appropriate resource allocation and data management.
expected_output:
To determine the appropriate handling strategy for an event based on its 'event.kind' value, you would consider the high-level information that 'event.kind' provides about the type of information the event contains. Different 'event.kind' values may warrant different handling strategies. For example:

- **alert**: Events classified as alerts typically indicate a security issue or anomaly that requires immediate attention. Handling strategies might include triggering automated responses, sending notifications to security teams, and initiating investigations.

- **metric**: Metric events are used to measure and report quantitative data over time, such as CPU usage or memory consumption. Handling strategies might involve aggregating data for monitoring purposes, setting up thresholds for alerts, and visualizing trends in dashboards.

- **state**: State change events represent changes in the state of a system or resource, such as a service starting or stopping. Handling strategies might include logging these changes for auditing purposes, updating system status dashboards, and triggering workflows based on specific state transitions.

- **event**: General events that do not fit into the other categories. Handling strategies might vary widely depending on the specific content of the event, but could include logging for audit purposes, triggering alerts based on certain conditions, or integrating with other systems for further processing.

Specific considerations influencing the decision might include the sensitivity of the data, the frequency of the events, the potential impact of the events, and compliance requirements. Different retention policies, access controls, and monitoring intervals might be applied based on these factors.
context:
| event.dataset | Name of the dataset. If an event source publishes more than one type of log or events (e.g. access log, error log), the dataset is used to specify which one the event comes from. It's recommended but not required to start the dataset name with the module name, followed by a dot, then the dataset name. | constant_keyword |
| event.kind | This is one of four ECS Categorization Fields, and indicates the highest level in the ECS category hierarchy. `event.kind` gives high-level information about what type of information the event contains, without being specific to the contents of the event. For example, values of this field distinguish alert events from metric events. The value of this field can be used to inform how these kinds of events should be handled. They may warrant different retention, different access control, it may also help understand whether the data is coming in at a regular interval or not. | keyword |

They may warrant different retention, different access control, it may also help understand whether the data is coming in at a regular interval or not. | keyword |

    - name: sqs
      type: group
      fields:
        - name: oldest_message_age.sec
          type: long
          metric_type: gauge
          format: duration

            The maximum approximate age of the oldest non-deleted message in the queue.
        - name: messages.delayed
          type: long
          metric_type: gauge
          description: |
            The average number of messages in the queue that are delayed and not available for reading immediately.
        - name: messages.not_visible
          type: long
          metric_type: gauge

            The average number of messages that are in flight.
        - name: messages.visible
          type: long
          metric_type: gauge
          description: |

Valid values: An integer from 0 to 900 (15 minutes). Default: 0.

  • MaximumMessageSize \u2013 The limit of how many bytes a message can contain before Amazon SQS rejects it. Valid values: An integer from 1,024 bytes (1 KiB) up to 262,144 bytes (256 KiB). Default: 262,144 (256 KiB).

  • MessageRetentionPeriod \u2013 The length of time, in seconds, for which Amazon SQS retains a message. Valid values: An integer representing seconds, from 60 (1 minute) to 1,209,600 (14 days). Default: 345,600 (4 days). When you change a queue's attributes, the change can take up to 60 seconds for most of the attributes to propagate throughout the Amazon SQS system. Changes made to the MessageRetentionPeriod attribute can take up to 15 minutes and will impact existing messages in the queue potentially causing them to be expired and deleted if the MessageRetentionPeriod is reduced below the age of existing messages.

For each AWS service you enable metrics data collection for, the AWS integration will collect metrics in all the AWS regions where there are available metrics for that service. The collection period is also set to sensible defaults

## Data streams

The AWS CloudWatch integration collects two types of data: logs and metrics.

**Logs** help you keep a record of different services in AWS, like EC2, RDS, and S3.
The log data stream includes the CloudWatch log message along with contextual information. See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of different services in AWS, like EC2, RDS, and S3.
The metric data stream includes the metrics that are returned from a CloudWatch API query along with contextual information. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

input: Given the context provided, if you were to analyze metrics related to Amazon Simple Queue Service (SQS), how would you determine which specific SQS queue's metrics are being represented by the field 'aws.dimensions.QueueName', and what does this imply about the data you can gather from these metrics?
actual_output: The field `aws.dimensions.QueueName` specifies the exact SQS queue name, allowing you to filter metrics to that specific queue. This implies you can gather queue-specific data, such as message throughput, latency, or error rates, tied to the identified queue.
expected_output: To determine which specific SQS queue's metrics are being represented by the field 'aws.dimensions.QueueName', you would look at the value associated with this field in the metric data. Each unique value of 'aws.dimensions.QueueName' corresponds to a different SQS queue. This implies that the data you can gather from these metrics is specific to each SQS queue, providing insights such as the total number of ReceiveMessage API calls that did not return a message ('aws.sqs.empty_receives'), the average number of messages in the queue that are delayed ('aws.sqs.messages.delayed'), and the total number of messages deleted from the queue ('aws.sqs.messages.deleted'). By analyzing these metrics for each queue, you can monitor the performance and usage patterns of individual SQS queues.

input: What specific types of data, including metrics and logs, does the Amazon EMR integration collect to provide insights into the state and events happening within Amazon EMR?
actual_output: The Amazon EMR integration collects **metrics** such as cluster progress, cluster state, cluster/node storage, and **logs** like cluster status, node status details, and events. These provide insights into EMR's state and operational events.
expected_output: The Amazon EMR integration collects two types of data: metrics and logs. Metrics provide insight into the state of Amazon EMR, including cluster progress, cluster state, and cluster or node storage. Logs help keep a record of events happening in Amazon EMR, such as cluster status and node status details.

## Data streams

The AWS VPN integration collects one type of data: metrics.

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of your AWS-hosted VPNs.
Metrics collected by the Amazon VPN integration include the unique host ID, the host IP addresses, the name of the domain of which the host is a member, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

* **AWS Credentials** to connect with your AWS account.
* **AWS Permissions** to make sure the user you're using to connect has permission to share the relevant data.

For more details about these requirements, please take a look at the [AWS integration documentation](https://docs.elastic.co/integrations/aws#requirements).

# Amazon EMR (ElasticMapReduce)

The Amazon EMR integration allows you to monitor [Amazon EMR](https://aws.amazon.com/emr/) — a fully managed big data processing and analytics service.

Use the Amazon EMR integration to collect metrics related to your EMR instances. Then visualize that data in Kibana, create alerts to notify you if something goes wrong, and reference the metrics when troubleshooting an issue.

For example, you could use this data to track Amazon EMR cluster progress and cluster storage. Then you can alert when utilization for an instance crosses a predefined threshold.

**IMPORTANT: Extra AWS charges on API requests will be generated by this integration. Check [API Requests](https://www.elastic.co/docs/current/integrations/aws#api-requests) for more details.**

## Data streams

The Amazon EMR integration collects two types of data: metrics and logs.

## Data streams

The Amazon ELB integration collects two types of data: logs and metrics.

**Logs** help you keep a record of events happening in Amazon ELB.
Logs collected by the Amazon ELB integration include the time a request was received, the client's IP address, latencies, request paths, server responses, and more. See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of Amazon ELB.
Metrics collected by the Amazon ELB integration include the host name, IP address, average latency, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

Before using any AWS integration you will need:

## Data streams

The Amazon EC2 integration collects two types of data: logs and metrics.

**Logs** help you keep a record of events happening in Amazon EC2.
Logs collected by the Amazon EC2 integration include the region in which an instance is running, the operating system architecture, container information, and more. See more details in the [Logs reference](#logs-reference).

**Metrics** give you insight into the state of your Amazon EC2 instances.
Metrics collected by the Amazon EC2 integration include the Amazon EC2 instance ID, the number of earned CPU credits that an instance has accrued since it was launched or started, and more. See more details in the [Metrics reference](#metrics-reference).

## Requirements

You need Elasticsearch for storing and searching your data and Kibana for visualizing and managing it.
You can use our hosted Elasticsearch Service on Elastic Cloud, which is recommended, or self-manage the Elastic Stack on your own hardware.

input: What specific values does the 'aws.vpn.metrics.TunnelState.avg' metric indicate for static VPNs, and what do these values represent?
actual_output:
For static VPNs, the **aws.vpn.metrics.TunnelState.avg** metric uses:  
- **0** = Tunnel **DOWN**  
- **1** = Tunnel **UP**  

These values represent the operational state of the VPN tunnel.
expected_output: For static VPNs, the 'aws.vpn.metrics.TunnelState.avg' metric indicates that a value of 0 represents the DOWN state, and a value of 1 represents the UP state.
